channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: China’s G.D.P. Stronger Than Expected, Led by Infrastructure Spending
item.description: A steep slide in housing prices has left consumers less prosperous and less willing to spend, but the government is pouring money into new rail lines and other projects.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.media:description: An import and export trade show in southern China this week.

item.title: Sneaker Company Allbirds Plans to Pivot to A.I. Yes, A.I.
item.description: After selling its business for $39 million last month, the company said it planned to buy powerful computer chips and rebrand itself NewBird AI.
item.dc:creator: Livia Albeck-Ripka
item.media:credit: Jeenah Moon for The New York Times
item.media:description: The shoe company Allbirds announced on Wednesday that it would “pivot its business” to artificial intelligence.

item.title: 5 Takeaways From the Live Nation Antitrust Trial
item.description: A jury found that the concert giant operated as a monopoly, a verdict that could have major reverberations in the music industry.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:description: A judge will now determine damages and remedies in the case, which could involve divestments or a breakup of the company.

item.title: U.K.’s Starmer Summons U.S. Social Media Companies Over Child Safety Online
item.description: The British prime minister will meet with executives from Meta, Google and other companies on Thursday as his government explores how to protect children from online harms.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Prime Minister Keir Starmer of Britain speaking about online safety at a school in London last month.

item.title: Ford Says Doug Field, Who Led Company's E.V. Push, is Leaving
item.description: Doug Field, a former Tesla and Apple executive, had returned to Ford Motor in 2021 to help develop new electric models and software.
item.dc:creator: Neal E. Boudette
item.media:credit: Emily Elconin for The New York Times
item.media:description: Doug Field, a top electric vehicle executive, will leave Ford Motor next month.

item.title: Live Nation and Ticketmaster Illegally Monopolized Ticketing Market, Jury Finds
item.description: In a verdict that could have far-reaching consequences in the music industry, the live colossus that includes Ticketmaster was found to have violated antitrust laws.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:description: Live Nation faced allegations of antitrust violations in court even though the federal government settled with the company soon after the proceedings began.

item.title: Trump’s Quest for More Sway Over Fed Faces Fresh Hurdles
item.description: Jerome H. Powell’s term as chair of the Federal Reserve ends on May 15, but it appears increasingly likely that he will stay on in some capacity at the central bank after that.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jerome Powell, chair of the Federal Reserve, said that if the Senate did not confirm a successor by May 15, he would stay on as “chair pro tempore.”

item.title: S&P 500 Hits Record High as Stock Market Looks Beyond Iran War
item.description: Investors appear to be treating an end to the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran as a foregone conclusion, as the S&P 500 closes above 7,000.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Snap Is Laying Off 16% of Full-Time Staff as It Embraces A.I.
item.description: The company, which owns the social media app Snapchat, said it was laying off about 1,000 employees as it increased its reliance on artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone
item.media:description: “We believe that rapid advancements in artificial intelligence enable our teams to reduce repetitive work, increase velocity, and better support our community, partners, and advertisers,” Evan Spiegel, Snap’s chief executive, said in a memo.

item.title: Debt Alarms Ring as Countries Rack Up More Emergency Spending
item.description: Amid signs of a prolonged period of high energy costs, policymakers are urging restraint as governments open up the public till to protect households and businesses.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.media:description: Chancellor Friedrich Merz of Germany, second from left, with other officials at a news briefing in Berlin this week.

item.title: Iran-Linked Ships Slow or Stop as U.S. Navy Enforces Strait of Hormuz Blockade
item.description: More than a dozen U.S. Navy warships are enforcing a blockade on all vessels from all nations entering or leaving coastal areas or ports in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A vessel in the Strait of Hormuz on Sunday. Traffic is still at a fraction of its usual levels.

item.title: That Meeting You Hate May Keep A.I. From Stealing Your Job
item.description: As artificial intelligence makes many tasks easier, the human work of cajoling, arm-twisting and reassuring appears to be rising in importance.
item.dc:creator: Noam Scheiber

item.title: Amid Iran War, Companies Look to Extend a Record Profit Run by Raising Prices
item.description: Higher inflation is leading companies to raise prices without sacrificing margins.
item.dc:creator: Talmon Joseph Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Johnson & Johnson, the drug and medical-device maker, reported strong earnings and boosted its full-year earnings forecast.

item.title: Trump Threatens to Fire Powell if He Does Not Resign From Fed
item.description: Although Jerome H. Powell’s term as Fed chair officially ends on May 15, he can stay on as a governor until 2028 and said he would not leave until a criminal investigation was over.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jerome H. Powell has said that he has “no intention of leaving the board” of the Fed until a criminal investigation into his handling of renovations at the central bank’s headquarters was “well and truly over.”

item.title: Trump Threatens to Fire Powell, Again
item.description: The new threat against the Fed chair comes ahead of a Senate confirmation hearing for Kevin Warsh, President Trump’s pick to lead the central bank.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Vivienne Walt
item.media:description: President Trump again endorsed a criminal investigation over the renovation of the Federal Reserve headquarters.

item.title: Trump Increased Tax Refunds, but a Political Challenge Still Looms
item.description: While last year’s tax law has raised the average I.R.S. refund, the boost is less than the White House expected — and may not have much impact on voters.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:description: Frank Bisignano, the leader of the Internal Revenue Service, is scheduled to testify about this year’s filing season in the Senate on Wednesday.

item.title: Japan Pledges $10 Billion to Help Countries Cope With Oil Prices
item.description: With supply chains at risk, the aid to Southeast Asian nations is aimed at securing the availability of oil-based products, including medical equipment.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis and Kiuko Notoya
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, speaking in Tokyo on Wednesday, said Japan “will work together with Asian countries to build a resilient energy and critical mineral supply chain.”

item.title: 11 Electric Cars Worth a Look as Gas Prices Soar
item.description: The war in Iran has auto shoppers sizing up electrics. Many strong cars are on the market, and there are bargains to be had when buying used.
item.dc:creator: Tom Voelk
item.category: Cadillac Division of General Motors Corp
item.media:credit: Lauren Justice for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Tesla supercharger station in Barstow, Calif. High gas prices are pushing car buyers to consider switching to electrics.

item.title: How Older Adults Are Using VR to Counter Social Isolation
item.description: New tools tailored for use in senior living communities allow for shared experiences and social bonding.
item.dc:creator: Charley Locke
item.media:credit: Morgan Lieberman for The New York Times

item.title: War Exposes Gaps in India’s Clean Energy Push
item.description: The country’s creaking electricity grid is lagging behind its push for renewable sources — just as its growing economy hungers for oil stranded by war.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli and Suhasini Raj
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Saumya Khandelwal for The New York Times
item.media:description: A floating solar farm in Visakhapatnam, India.

item.title: Prosecutors Make Surprise Visit to Fed as Pirro Defends Investigation
item.description: The Justice Department’s criminal inquiry into the Federal Reserve threatens to delay the confirmation of the next chair.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith and Glenn Thrush
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: An overview showing construction at the Federal Reserve last year. The $2.5 billion renovation project is at the center of an investigation into the central bank.

item.title: US Blockade Stops Iran-Linked Ships From Crossing Strait of Hormuz
item.description: U.S. Central Command said that six vessels had complied with directions to turn around and re-enter an Iranian port.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Vessels anchored in Muscat, Oman, last month. U.S. Central Command said a blockade on Iranian-linked ships would be enforced from the Gulf of Oman and the Arabian Sea, both east of the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: N.F.L. Reporter Resigns From The Athletic Amid an Investigation
item.description: The publication, which is owned by The New York Times, was investigating the conduct of Dianna Russini after photographs showed her with the head coach of the New England Patriots.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:description: Dianna Russini joined The Athletic in 2023 and was a co-host of the “Scoop City” podcast.

item.title: Amazon Buys Globalstar for $10.8 Billion, Movingto Expand Its Satellite Internet Service
item.description: The e-commerce giant is buying Globalstar, a satellite communications company, as it aims to expand its own satellite internet service to compete with Elon Musk’s Starlink.
item.dc:creator: Natallie Rocha
item.media:description: A United Launch Alliance Atlas V rocket lifting off from the Cape Canaveral Space Force Station last year, carrying Amazon’s Project Kuiper internet network satellites.

item.title: Bessent Questions the Cause of Climate Change and Its Economic Toll
item.description: The Treasury secretary said it is “difficult to deconstruct” the reasons for global warming, which he described as a belief of the “elite.”
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport and Lisa Friedman
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, right, said on Tuesday with the Danish academic Bjorn Lomborg that it was difficult to determine the causes of climate change.

item.title: Pittsburgh Post-Gazette Saved From Closure by Nonprofit
item.description: The Venetoulis Institute of Local Journalism, which operates The Baltimore Banner, has bought The Post-Gazette. The newspaper was set to shut down next month.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:credit: Alex Welsh for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We want to provide high-quality independent journalism to communities that need it the most,” said Stewart W. Bainum Jr., who finances the Venetoulis Institute for Local Journalism.

item.title: Ricky Cobb Has Built a Mini-Empire Through ’70s Nostalgia
item.description: Ricky Cobb has built a big online following with his irreverent postings about the absurdities of the 1970s.
item.dc:creator: Joe Drape
item.media:credit: Lyndon French for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ricky Cobb’s fans say he celebrates an era in American life that seems less uptight and more fun.

item.title: JPMorgan Sees Resilience in U.S. Economy Despite Iran War
item.description: The nation’s largest bank said it earned record trading profits in the first quarter but tempered expectations overall for its business.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland and Stacy Cowley
item.media:description: Jamie Dimon, the chief executive of JPMorgan Chase, said that the risks going forward were “not minor.”

item.title: Kevin Warsh, Trump’s Pick for Fed Chair, Discloses Vast Wealth Ahead of Confirmation Hearing
item.description: Kevin M. Warsh vowed to divest a substantial amount of his more than $100 million in assets as he faces a complicated path to becoming the next chair of the Federal Reserve.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith, Tony Romm and Steven Rich
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh will be at a Senate hearing next week to consider him as the next chair of the Federal Reserve.

item.title: Middle East War Will Slow Global Economic Growth, I.M.F. Warns
item.description: The conflict could also fuel another bout of inflation, according to the International Monetary Fund.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: New International Monetary Fund projections showed a global economy that was stopped in its tracks by the U.S. war in Iran.

item.title: What’s Driving the Wartime Stock Rebound
item.description: The S&P 500 has recouped all its losses since the Middle East war started on Feb. 28, as a bullish “vibe shift” takes hold among some investors.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount and Grady McGregor
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Questions swirl about the fate of the Strait of Hormuz, but that uncertainty isn’t bothering investors.

item.title: Oil Prices Drop and Stocks Approach Highs as Investors Eye Possible Path to Peace
item.description: Investors saw a possible path to peace, with the United States and Iran trading proposals for Iran to suspend nuclear activity.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Trump’s Latest Oil Blockade Brings Bigger Economic Risks
item.description: Oil markets shrugged it off, but the effort to hurt Iran could provoke retaliation that inflicts more damage on energy assets and the global economy.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A strike on the Bapco oil refinery in Bahrain in early March. A U.S. blockade of the Strait of Hormuz may give Iran a reason to restart attacks on energy assets throughout the Persian Gulf.

item.title: Detroit Aims to Become Center of U.S. Drone Manufacturing
item.description: The state, known for making cars, is spending millions to entice drone manufacturers that can bring new business to auto suppliers.
item.dc:creator: Farah Stockman and Sylvia Jarrus
item.media:description: “Frankly, we wouldn’t be able to afford 420,000 square feet in downtown San Francisco,” said Keith Miao, the founder of Birdstop, a drone start-up in Detroit.

item.title: New Rules Hinder Foreign Firms From Moving Supply Chains From China
item.description: Multinationals in China are concerned that the regulations could allow authorities to penalize companies and executives for shifting supply chains away from the country.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.media:description: Steel pipes awaiting export in Lianyungang, China. The country’s trade surplus reached nearly $1.2 trillion last year as exports continued to outpace imports.

item.title: Netflix Leader Makes Rare Overture to Cinema Owners
item.description: Ted Sarandos attended a major movie theater conference for the first time and met with domestic and international owners, people familiar with the meetings said.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling
item.media:credit: Bryan Anselm for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ted Sarandos, Netflix’s co-chief executive, in January. He held two separate meetings on Sunday with top domestic and international theater owners, four people said.

item.title: Two Iran-Linked Ships Passed Through Strait of Hormuz Ahead of U.S. Blockade
item.description: The vessels exited the Strait of Hormuz on Monday, hours before a U.S. naval blockade took effect.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Strait of Hormuz last week. Traffic in the waterway has slowed since the start of the conflict.

item.title: Shipping Companies Remain Reluctant to Sail Through Strait of Hormuz
item.description: The United States said it has started to block vessels doing business with Iran from the strait. Details on how that would work are unclear.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis and Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Vessels at the Strait of Hormuz, off the coast of Oman on Sunday.

item.title: Trump Administration’s Temporary Reprieve on Russian Oil Expires
item.description: A sanctions waiver aimed at keeping global oil prices down had allowed Moscow to sell oil currently at sea.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An oil terminal in the port of St. Petersburg, Russia.

item.title: Judge Dismisses Trump’s Suit Over WSJ Report on Birthday Card to Epstein
item.description: The judge said President Trump had not “plausibly alleged” that The Journal published the article with actual malice.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:credit: Juan Arredondo for The New York Times
item.media:description: A federal judge dismissed President Trump’s defamation lawsuit against The Wall Street Journal, saying that the complaint did not plausibly show actual malice.

item.title: Iran Blockade Sets Up a Test of Which Side Can Endure More Pain
item.description: President Trump is trying to choke off the country’s lifeline with a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. But the Iranians are betting that his tolerance for political pain is limited.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Fuel prices reflected on a semi-truck in Aurora, Ore., last week. “Soon you’ll be nostalgic for $4 to $5 gas,” Iran’s top negotiator warned American consumers after peace talks fell apart over the weekend.

item.title: Hollywood Heavyweights Sign Letter Opposing Paramount’s Deal for Warner Bros.
item.description: The letter warns that the deal will result in fewer jobs for creatives, along with higher costs and less choice for audiences.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: A letter signed by more than 1,000 writers, actors and directors said Paramount’s deal for Warner Bros. Discovery deal would harm Hollywood’s already distressed entertainment industry.

item.title: Goldman Sachs Earnings Show Effects of Iran War
item.description: It will be a tricky earnings season, as the investment banking giant became the first to show on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: David Solomon, the chief executive of Goldman Sachs, said that the geopolitical backdrop had become “very complex.”

item.title: Investors Brace for a New Hormuz Blockade Threat
item.description: President Trump’s plan to bar ships from entering or leaving Iranian ports has put markets on edge and added to global economic uncertainty.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump’s blockade threat has jolted global markets again.

item.title: The Battle Over Refreshers Heats Up as McDonald’s Enters the Mix
item.description: The fast-food giant will add fruit-flavored drinks to its menus next month as chains like Dunkin’ and Starbucks sell more cold drinks than hot ones.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times

item.title: The Economist Is Putting Names (and Faces) to Its Magazine
item.description: Nameless no more, writers for The Economist are mixing it up on video from its studio in London.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Sam Bush for The New York Times

item.title: How Iran, Suffering Under Sanctions, Diversified Its Economy
item.description: As the nation contended with high inflation, high unemployment and unrest before the war, it became more than just an oil exporter.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen and Robert Gebeloff
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: China’s Electrostate Is Poised to Win From War in the Middle East
item.description: A cluster of Chinese firms is poised to win big from the energy crisis set off by the war in the Middle East, building on booming investment in artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Keith Bradsher
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Gilles Sabrié for The New York Times
item.media:description: An electricity transmission hub is at the terminus in south-central China of the world’s longest and most powerful ultrahigh-voltage power line. Chinese companies dominate the manufacturing of nearly every piece of a modern grid.

item.title: Elon Musk, Who Owns X, Appears to Post on TikTok
item.description: A verified account with the @elonmusk handle also recently showed up on Instagram, as the billionaire prepares to take his rocket company SpaceX public.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac
item.media:description: Elon Musk has previously shown disdain for other social media companies that X competes against.

item.title: Oil Prices Are Pressured and Stocks Gain After Trump Revives Talk of a Deal
item.description: Oil prices surged as markets reopened following news that U.S. peace talks with Iran had ended without a deal.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
